name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
```

```english
Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَ تَقُولُونَ مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٢)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Why do you say what you do not do? (2)
```

```arabic
كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٣)
```

```english
It is greatly outrageous to Allah that you should say what you do not do. (3)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُم بُنْيَـٰنٌ مَّرْصُوصٌ (٤)
```

```english
Indeed Allah loves those who fight in His way in ranks, as if they were a compact structure. (4)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِى وَقَد تَّعْلَمُونَ أَنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا زَاغُوٓا۟ أَزَاغَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٥)
```

```english
When Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! Why do you torment me, when you certainly know that I am Allah’s apostle to you?’ So when they swerved [from the right path], Allah made their hearts swerve, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (5)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُم مُّصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَمُبَشِّرًۢا بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِى ٱسْمُهُۥٓ أَحْمَدُ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٦)
```

```english
And when Jesus son of Mary said, ‘O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, to confirm what is before me of the Torah, and to give the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad.’ But when he brought them manifest proofs, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’ (6)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُوَ يُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧)
```

```english
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates falsehoods against Allah, while he is being summoned to Islam? And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (7)
```

```arabic
يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَٱللَّهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨)
```

```english
They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. (8)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ (٩)
```

```english
It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions though the polytheists should be averse. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah encourages the believers and incites them to strive in the way of Allah and to fight the enemies of His religion. It informs them that this religion is a radiant light (نور) from Allah, the Glorified, which the disbelievers (الكفار) from among the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) wish to extinguish with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light even if the disbelievers (الكافرون) detest it, and He will make it prevail over all religions even if the polytheists (المشركون) detest it.

It further conveys that this Prophet in whom they have believed is a Messenger from Allah, whom He sent with guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and the religion of truth (دين الحق), and that Jesus, son of Mary (عليه السلام), gave glad tidings of him to the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل).

Therefore, the believers must strengthen their resolve to obey him and comply with what he commands them regarding struggle (الجهاد) and aiding Allah in His religion, so that Allah may grant them felicity in their Hereafter, grant them victory, grant them openings in their worldly life, and support them against their enemies.

They must also not say that which they do not do, nor retreat from what they promise, for that necessitates detestation (مقت) from Allah the Exalted and causes harm to the Messenger. In this lies the danger that Allah might cause their hearts to swerve, just as He did with the people of Moses (عليه السلام) when they harmed him while knowing he was the Messenger of Allah to them; and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing (الظالمين) people.

The Surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." (61:1). The interpretation of this has been provided previously. The discourse opens with glorification (التسبيح) because it contains a rebuke of the believers for saying what they do not do, and a warning to them of Allah’s detestation and His causing the hearts of the transgressors (الفاسقين) to swerve.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَ تَقُولُونَ ما لا تَفْعَلُونَ** — "O you who have faith! Why do you say what you do not do?" (61:2). The word **لِمَ** is a contraction of **لما**, where the **ما** is interrogative (استفهامية) and the **ل** is for causation (للتعليل). The speech is directed as a rebuke (توبيخ); thus, it contains a rebuke of the believers for saying what they do not do. No heed should be paid to the claim of some exegetes that the "those who believe" intended here are the hypocrites (المنافقون), and that the rebuke is for them rather than the believers due to the latter's high status.

This is because of the abundance of verses containing rebukes and admonishments of the believers, especially in the verses revealed concerning battles and related events, such as Uhud, al-Ahzab, Hunayn, the Peace of Hudaybiyyah, Tabuk, spending in the way of Allah, and others. The righteous (الصالحون) among these believers only became righteous in soul and exalted in status through the divine cultivation (التربية الإلهية) contained in such gradual rebukes and admonishments directed at them; they did not possess such qualities inherently from themselves.

Although the subject of the rebuke, according to the literal sense of the verse, is the general failure of action to match speech, the breaking of promises, and the violation of covenants—which is indeed the case as it is a manifestation of the outward (الظاهر) contradicting the inward (الباطن), which is hypocrisy (النفاق)—the context of the verses, including His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِهِ صَفًّا** (Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His way in ranks), and what follows in His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى تِجارَةٍ** (O you who have faith! Shall I guide you to a commerce...), etc., indicates that the specific object of the rebuke was the failure of some of them to fulfill their promise of steadfastness in battle and not retreating or fleeing, or their sluggishness, or their failure to go out for battle, or their failure to spend on equipping themselves or others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَبُرَ مَقْتاً عِنْدَ اللهِ أَنْ تَقُولُوا ما لا تَفْعَلُونَ** — "It is most hateful to Allah that you should say what you do not do." (61:3). **Detestation (المقت)** is intense hatred (البغض الشديد). The verse serves as a causal explanation (تعليل) for the content of the previous verse. Thus, He, the Exalted, hates for a human being to say what he does not do because it is a form of hypocrisy. For a person to say what he does not do is different from not doing what he says; the first is hypocrisy, while the second is a weakness of will (الإرادة) and a frailty of resolve (العزم). This is a vice (رذيلة) contrary to the felicity (سعادة) of the human soul, for Allah has built the felicity of the human soul upon performing good and acquiring virtue through the path of choice (الاختيار), the key to which is resolve and will. Only firm resolve and will have an effect; the failure of action to follow speech is an effect of frail resolve and weak will, and no good or felicity can be hoped for a person in such a state.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِهِ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُمْ بُنْيانٌ مَرْصُوصٌ** — "Indeed Allah loves those who fight in His way in ranks, as if they were a compact structure." (61:4). **The rank (الصف)** is to place things in a straight line, like people or trees, as stated by al-Raghib. It is a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle (اسم الفاعل), which is why it is not pluralized. It is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun of the subject in "they fight" (يُقاتِلُونَ). The meaning is: they fight in His way while they are in ranks.

**The structure (البنيان)** refers to a building (البناء), and **compact (المرصوص)** is derived from lead (الرصاص). What is intended is a building strengthened with lead so that it resists whatever causes of destruction might strike it.

The verse provides a causal explanation for the specific instance—which is promising steadfastness in battle and then fleeing—by implication (بالالتزام), just as the previous verse explains the rebuke for the general act of saying what one does not do. This is because if Allah, the Glorified, loves those who fight and remain in their positions without wavering, it necessarily follows that He hates those who promise to stand firm and then flee when they are present on the battlefield.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِي وَقَدْ تَعْلَمُونَ أَنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ** — "And when Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! Why do you hurt me, when you know that I am Allah’s apostle to you?’" (61:5), etc. In the verse is an allusion to the Children of Israel harming their Messenger, Moses (عليه السلام), and their obstinacy, until it resulted in Allah causing their hearts to swerve. In this is an implied prohibition (نهي التزامي) for the believers against harming the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), lest their affair end up like that of the people of Moses regarding the swerving of hearts. Indeed, He, the Exalted, has said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً مُهِيناً** — "Indeed those who offend Allah and His Apostle are cursed by Allah in the world and the Hereafter, and He has prepared for them a humiliating punishment." (33:57).

The verse, with its implied prohibition, carries the same meaning as His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ آذَوْا مُوسى فَبَرَّأَهُ اللهُ مِمَّا قالُوا وَكانَ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَجِيهاً يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً** — "O you who have faith! Do not be like those who offended Moses, whereat Allah cleared him of what they alleged, and he was distinguished in Allah’s sight. O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words." (33:69-70).

The context of these two verses and the mention of clearing Moses (عليه السلام) indicates that the harm intended—from which Allah cleared him—was not their disobedience to his commands or their rebellion against his authority, for there would be no meaning to "clearing" him in that case. Rather, it was that they slandered him (عليه السلام) and said things about him that were shameful and disgraceful, causing him pain, so Allah cleared him of what they said and attributed to him. His saying in the following verse: "Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words" supports what we have mentioned.

This is further supported by His, the Exalted, allusion to some instances of harming the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by word or deed in His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتَ النَّبِيِّ إِلَّا أَنْ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ إِلى طَعامٍ غَيْرَ ناظِرِينَ إِناهُ وَلكِنْ إِذا دُعِيتُمْ فَادْخُلُوا فَإِذا طَعِمْتُمْ فَانتَشِرُوا وَلا مُسْتَأْنِسِينَ لِحَدِيثٍ إِنَّ ذلِكُمْ كانَ يُؤْذِي النَّبِيَّ** — "O you who have faith! Do not enter the Prophet’s houses for a meal unless you are granted leave, without waiting for it to be readied. But when you are invited, enter, and when you have eaten, disperse, without lingering for the sake of talk. That used to offend the Prophet..." until He says: **وَإِذا سَأَلْتُمُوهُنَّ مَتاعاً فَسْئَلُوهُنَّ مِنْ وَراءِ حِجابٍ** — "...And when you ask [his womenfolk] for any object, ask them from behind a curtain..." until He says: **وَما كانَ لَكُمْ أَنْ تُؤْذُوا رَسُولَ اللهِ وَلا أَنْ تَنْكِحُوا أَزْواجَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ أَبَداً إِنَّ ذلِكُمْ كانَ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَظِيماً** — "...You should not offend the Apostle of Allah, nor may you ever marry his wives after him. Indeed that would be a grave [sin] with Allah." (33:53).

In summary, His saying: **وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ** (And when Moses said to his people...), etc., contains a hint toward the prohibition of harming the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by word or deed while knowing [his status], just as the end of the verse contains a threat and warning that this is a transgression (فسق) that may lead to Him, the Exalted, causing the heart of the one who commits it to swerve.

His saying: **فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ** — "So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve, and Allah does not guide the transgressing people." (61:5). **Swerving (الزيغ)** is a deviation from uprightness, and its necessary consequence is a turning away from truth (الحق) toward falsehood (الباطل).

His causing them to swerve consists of withholding His mercy and cutting off His guidance (هداية) from them, as indicated by the causal explanation in His saying: "and Allah does not guide the transgressing people," where He explains the swerving by the absence of guidance. This is a swerving by way of retribution (المجازاة) and a consolidation of the swerving they first engaged in due to their transgression, which necessitated retribution, as He, the Exalted, said: **يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "...He misleads many thereby, and guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors." (2:26). It is not an initial swerving or an original misleading, which would be unworthy of His holy presence (ساحة قدسه).

From this, the error of the claim that it is not permissible for "Allah made their hearts swerve" to mean swerving from faith (الإيمان) becomes apparent. The claim is based on the idea that it is not permissible for Allah to make anyone swerve from faith, and also that if it meant swerving from faith, the speech would lose its utility, because if they have already swerved from faith, they have become disbelievers, so there would be no meaning to saying Allah made them swerve from faith.

The reason this is erroneous is that the statement "it is not permissible for Him, the Exalted, to make anyone swerve from faith" is rejected in its absolute sense. The criterion here is the necessity of injustice (الظلم), and injustice only occurs if the swerving and misleading were initial. As for that which is by way of retribution—the reality of which is the withholding of mercy and the cutting off of guidance because the servant caused it through his transgression and turning away from mercy and guidance—there is no evidence for its prohibition, neither rationally nor through transmitted texts.

As for the claim that the speech would lose its utility, it is refuted by the fact that the swerving attributed to the servant, through which disbelief (الكفر) occurs, is the actualization of what he earned through transgression. That which is attributed to Him, the Exalted, is the consolidation of that swerving in the servant's heart and the sealing of it. Thus, the servant's swerving from faith due to his transgression and the resulting disbelief does not negate Allah's consolidation of that swerving and disbelief in his heart as a retribution.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ قالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ وَمُبَشِّراً بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِي اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ** — "And when Jesus son of Mary said, ‘O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah, and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad.’" (61:6). It was mentioned at the beginning of the discourse that this verse, the one before it, and the three verses following it are intended to establish that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a Messenger whose messengership is known to the believers, whom Allah sent with guidance and the religion of truth to make it prevail over all religions even if the disbelievers from among the People of the Book detest it; and that the religion he brought is a radiant light from Allah which the polytheists wish to extinguish with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light even if the polytheists detest it.

Therefore, the believers must not harm him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while they know he is the Messenger of Allah to them, and they must support him and strive in the way of their Lord to revive His religion and spread His word.

From this, it is understood that His saying: "And when Jesus son of Mary said, ‘O Children of Israel!’" etc., is like a preamble to what will be mentioned regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a Messenger previously heralded, whom Allah sent with guidance and the religion of truth, and whose religion is His light by which people are guided.

That which He, the Exalted, narrated from Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام)—namely his saying: "O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah, and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad"—is a summary of his mission. He announced the origin of his mission with his saying: "Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you," indicating that he has no status other than being the bearer of a message from Allah to them. Then he explained the core of what he was sent to convey in his message with his saying: "confirming what is before me of the Torah, and giving the good news of an apostle..." etc.

His saying: "confirming what is before me of the Torah" is an explanation that his mission does not differ from the religion of the Torah nor contradict its law (شريعة); rather, it confirms it and did not abrogate any of its rulings except a few. Abrogation (النسخ) is an explanation of the end of a ruling's duration and is not an invalidation. Therefore, he (عليه السلام) combined the confirmation of the Torah with the abrogation of some of its rulings in what Allah narrated of his saying: **وَمُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...and to confirm what is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful to you some of what was forbidden you..." (3:50). He did not clarify for them except some of that wherein they differed, as in the narrated saying: **قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ** — "...I have come to you with wisdom, and to clarify for you some of what you differ about. So be wary of Allah and obey me." (43:63).

His saying: "and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad" refers to the second half of his (عليه السلام) message, having referred to the first half with his saying: "confirming what is before me of the Torah."

It is well known that **glad tidings (البشرى)** is news that pleases and delights the recipient, and it can only be regarding something good that will reach and benefit them. The good expected from the mission of a Prophet and his call is the opening of a door of divine mercy upon the people, containing the felicity of their worldly life and their Hereafter, whether through true belief, righteous deeds (عمل صالح), or both. Glad tidings of a Prophet after a Prophet, and of a new call after a previous call has arrived and established itself—given that the divine call is one, not invalidated by the passage of ages or the difference of days and nights—can only be conceived if the new call is more sublime in the true beliefs it contains and the laws that regulate the actions of society, and more comprehensive for the felicity of man in his worldly life and his Hereafter.

Through this explanation, it becomes clear that the meaning of his (عليه السلام) saying: "and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me..." etc., implies that what the Prophet Ahmad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) brought is more sublime and more perfect than what was contained in the Torah and what Jesus (عليه السلام) was sent with; he (عليه السلام) is an intermediary link between the two calls.

The meaning of his words: "Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming..." etc., returns to: I am a Messenger from Allah to you, calling to the law of the Torah and its path—and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you—and it is a law that Allah will complete by sending a Prophet who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad.

And so it is; for deep reflection on the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) to which Islam calls shows that it is more precise than what is in other previous heavenly laws, especially the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — to which it invites, which is the foundation of foundations upon which every ruling is built and to which every true knowledge returns. A portion of the discourse on this has preceded in the earlier discussions of this book.

Likewise, the practical laws and statutes did not leave anything, small or great, of human individual and social actions without regulating it, defining its limits, establishing it on the basis of monotheism, and directing it toward the goal of felicity.

To this is the allusion in His, the Exalted, saying: **الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبائِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلالَ الَّتِي كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "[those] who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them..." (7:157), and other verses describing the Quran.

The verse, namely His saying: "and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me," although explicit in the glad tidings, does not indicate that it is mentioned in his (عليه السلام) book. However, the verse of al-A'raf quoted just now: "whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel," as well as His saying regarding the description of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **ذلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ** — "...That is their description in the Torah, and their description in the Evangel..." (48:29), both indicate that.

His saying: **اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ** — "whose name is Ahmad": the indication of the context that Jesus (عليه السلام) referred to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as Ahmad, and that it was a name for him by which he was known among the people just as he was named Muhammad, is apparent and beyond doubt.

This is evidenced by the saying of Hassan [ibn Thabit]:

*The God and those who surround His Throne*
*And the pure ones have prayed upon the blessed Ahmad.*

And from the poetry of Abu Talib is his saying:

*And they said to Ahmad: You are a man*
*Of a deceptive tongue and weak means.*

*Indeed, Ahmad has come to them*
*With truth, and he did not come to them with lies.*

And his saying addressing al-Abbas, Hamza, Ja'far, and Ali, advising them to support the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم):

*May my mother and what she bore be a ransom for you*
*In being shields for the victory of Ahmad before all people.*

And from his poetry regarding him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), having named him by his other name, Muhammad:

*Did you not know that we found Muhammad*
*A Prophet like Moses, written in the first of books?*

It is gathered from this verse that they came across the existence of the glad tidings of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the heavenly books that were with the People of the Book at that time.

This is also supported by the faith of a group of the People of the Book from the Jews and Christians, among whom were some of their scholars like Abdullah ibn Salam and others. They used to hear these Quranic verses that mention the glad tidings of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his mention in the Torah and the Evangel, and they received them with acceptance, neither calling them lies nor showing any doubt or hesitation regarding them.

As for the absence of explicit glad tidings from Jesus in the Gospels circulating today, the Quran—which is a remaining miraculous sign—is in no need of their confirmation. The discussion regarding their chains of transmission and their reliability has preceded in the third volume of this book.

His saying: **فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ** — "But when he brought them manifest proofs, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’" (61:6). The pronoun in "he brought" (جاء) refers to Ahmad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the pronoun in "them" (جاءَهُمْ) refers to the Children of Israel, or to them and others. What is intended by "manifest proofs" (البينات) is the glad tidings, the miracle of the Quran, and other signs of prophethood.

The meaning is: when Ahmad, the one heralded, came to the Children of Israel, or came to them and others with clear signs, among which was the glad tidings of Jesus (عليه السلام), they said: "This is plain magic." It has also been recited as "This is a plain magician" (ساحر).

It has been said that the pronoun in "he brought" refers to Jesus (عليه السلام), but the context does not suit this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُوَ يُدْعى إِلَى الْإِسْلامِ** — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, while he is being invited to Islam?..." (61:7). The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار), and it is a rebuttal of their saying: "This is plain magic," for its meaning is that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is not a Messenger and that the religion of Allah he conveyed is not from Him, the Exalted.

What is intended by **Islam (الإسلام)** is the religion to which the Messenger of Allah calls, insofar as it is submission (تسليم) to Allah in what He wills and commands of belief and action. There is no doubt that the requirement of His, the Exalted, Lordship (ربوبيته) and Divinity (ألوهيته) is the absolute submission of His servants to Him. Thus, there is no doubt that the religion which is submission to Allah is His true religion by which one must abide; therefore, the claim that it is false and not from Allah is a fabrication against Allah.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: "while he is being invited to Islam" contains the proof that their saying: "This is plain magic" is a fabrication against Allah.

Fabrication (الافتراء) is a wrongdoing (ظلم) that the intellect does not doubt is wrongdoing, and the Law (الشرع) prohibits it. Wrongdoing is magnified by the greatness of the one against whom it is committed; so if it is against Allah, the Glorified, it is the greatest wrongdoing. Thus, none is more unjust than one who fabricates a lie against Allah.

The meaning is: none is more unjust than one who fabricates a lie against Allah—by denying the attribution of Allah's religion to Him—while he is being invited to the religion of Islam, which contains nothing but submission to Allah in what He willed, and there is no doubt that it is from Allah; and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ** — "They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths..." (61:8). **Extinguishing (إطفاء)** a light is to invalidate it and remove its radiance. Extinguishing light with mouths is done by blowing with them.

This verse occurs in Surah al-Tawbah, where it says: **يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ** (They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths). Al-Raghib said: He, the Exalted, said: "They desire to put out (أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا) the light of Allah" and "They desire to put out (لِيُطْفِؤُا) the light of Allah." The difference between the two places is that in His saying: "They desire to put out (أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا)," they intend the act of extinguishing Allah's light itself; whereas in His saying: "to put out (لِيُطْفِؤُا)," they intend a means through which they reach the extinguishing of Allah's light. [End of quote]. The summary is that the object of desire in "They desire to put out (أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا) the light of Allah" is the extinguishing itself, while in "They desire to put out (لِيُطْفِؤُا) the light of Allah," it is the cause leading to the extinguishing—which is blowing with the mouths—while the extinguishing is the purpose and goal.

The verse and the one following it are like an explanation of the meaning of what preceded in the previous verse regarding their wrongdoing in labeling the call as magic, and His, the Exalted, not guiding them because they are wrongdoers. The summary is that they intend to extinguish the light of Allah with the breath of their mouths, but Allah does not guide them to their objective; rather, He perfects His light and makes His religion prevail over all religions.

Thus, His saying: "They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths"—meaning by blowing with the mouths as a candle is extinguished with a breath—is a metaphor (كناية) for their assumption that the light of Allah, which is His religion, is a weak light like the light of a candle that can be extinguished with the slightest breath; so they labeled it as magic and denied its connection to Allah.

They were mistaken in their assumption, for it is the light of Allah which cannot be extinguished, and He has willed to perfect it even if the disbelievers detest it, and Allah brings His command to pass. This is His saying: **وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَلَوْ كره الْكافِرُونَ** — "...but Allah will perfect His light though the faithless should be averse." (61:8).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كره الْمُشْرِكُونَ** — "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse." (61:9). The genitive construction (الإضافة) in **دِينِ الْحَقِ** (the religion of truth) is explanatory (بيانية), as has been said. It appears that it is originally a possessive construction (إضافة لامية) with a subtle consideration: that for both truth and falsehood, there is a religion that each necessitates and to which each is specific. Allah, the Exalted, has approved the religion that belongs to the Truth—and He is the Truth, the Exalted—so He sent His Messenger.

Making something prevail (إظهار) over another is to aid it and make it overcome the other. What is intended by "all religions" (الدين كله) is every path followed other than the path of Allah, which is Islam. The verse serves as a causal explanation for His saying in the previous verse: "but Allah will perfect His light." The meaning is: Allah is the perfecter of His light because He is the one who sent His Messenger with His light, which is guidance and the religion of truth, to make it victorious over all religions, even if the polytheists from among the idolaters detest it.

It is gathered from the two verses that the religion of truth is the light of Allah on earth, just as that is gathered from His saying: **مَثَلُ نُورِهِ كَمِشْكاةٍ فِيها مِصْباحٌ** — "...The parable of His light is a niche wherein is a lamp..." (24:35), as has preceded in the interpretation of that verse.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِهِ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُمْ بُنْيانٌ مَرْصُوصٌ**, he said: "They stand in ranks like a structure that does not waver."

In *al-Majma'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِي وَقَدْ تَعْلَمُونَ أَنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ**, it is narrated in the story of Korah (قارون) that he bribed a woman against him—alleging that he had committed adultery with her—and they also accused him of killing Aaron (هارون).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمُبَشِّراً بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِي اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ**, he said: "Some of the Jews (may Allah curse them) asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Why were you named Ahmad, Muhammad, Bashir (bearer of glad tidings), and Nadhir (warner)?' He replied: 'As for Muhammad, I am praised (محمود) on earth. As for Ahmad, I am more praised (أحمد) in heaven than I am on earth. As for the Bashir, I give glad tidings of Paradise to whoever obeys Allah. As for the Nadhir, I warn whoever disobeys Allah of the Fire.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: regarding the verse, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from al-Irbad ibn Sariyah: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah in the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) and the Seal of the Prophets—while Adam was still kneaded in his clay—and I shall inform you of the interpretation of that: I am the prayer of Abraham, the glad tidings of Jesus to his people, and the vision of my mother which she saw, that a light came out from her by which the palaces of Syria were illuminated for her.'"

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain to Safwan ibn Yahya, the companion of the Sabiri, who said: "Abu Qurrah, the companion of the Catholicos, asked me to bring him to al-Rida (عليه السلام). I asked his permission for that, and he said: 'Bring him in to me.' When he entered, he kissed his carpet and said: 'This is what we are required to do in our religion with the nobles of the people of our time.'

Then he said: 'May Allah rectify you, what do you say about a group that made a claim, and another group of just witnesses testified for them?' He said: 'The claim is theirs.' He said: 'Then another group made a claim but found no witnesses other than themselves?' He said: 'They have nothing.'

He said: 'We have claimed that Jesus is the Spirit of Allah and His Word—and the Muslims have agreed with us on that. But the Muslims claimed that Muhammad is a Prophet, and we did not follow them in that. What we have agreed upon is better than what we have differed over.'

Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) said: 'What is your name?' He said: 'John (يوحنا).' He said: 'O John, we have believed in Jesus, the Spirit of Allah and His Word, who used to believe in Muhammad and give glad tidings of him, and who confessed for himself that he is a servant, sustained by a Lord. If the Jesus who is with you as the Spirit of Allah and His Word is not the one who believed in Muhammad and gave glad tidings of him, nor the one who confessed servitude to Allah, then we are quit of him. So where have we agreed?' He stood up and said to Safwan ibn Yahya: 'Get up, for we had no need of this gathering.'"

**I say:** It is as if he meant by his saying, "we had no need of this gathering," that his entry upon him (عليه السلام) did not benefit him since the argument he brought did not succeed.

In *Kamal al-Din*, with its chain to Ya'qub ibn Shu'ayb from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Between Jesus and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) there were five hundred years, of which two hundred and fifty years there was neither a Prophet nor a manifest scholar (عالم ظاهر).' I said: 'What were they?' He said: 'They were holding fast to the religion of Jesus (عليه السلام).' I said: 'What were they?' He said: 'They were believers.' Then he said: 'And there is never a time except that there is a scholar in it.'"

**I say:** What is intended by the "scholar" (العالم) is the Imam who is the Proof (الحجة). There are narrations concerning His saying: "They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths" and His saying: "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion" which mention that the light, guidance, and religion of truth is the guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام). This is from the perspective of conceptual flow and application (الجري والتطبيق) or from the inner meanings (البطن) and is not an [exclusive] interpretation. The counting of the interval between the Messiah and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as five hundred years contradicts what is famous in history, but researchers have mentioned that there is a disruption in the calendar of the birth [of Christ], and an allusion to that has passed in the third volume of this book.
